key_findings: Eval 0 (developer-project) remains strongly discriminating: 6/6 vs 1/6 — baseline produces standard project structure without routing tables, CONTEXT.md files, token budgets, or layer separation

Eval 1 (content-pipeline) is NOW DISCRIMINATING: 5/5 vs 3/5 — baseline lacks stage contracts (Inputs/Process/Outputs) and pipeline handoff specifications. The stochastic baseline no longer benefits from ICM training data leakage seen in iteration 1

Eval 2 (broken-workspace) is NOW NON-DISCRIMINATING: 5/5 vs 5/5 — baseline Sonnet produced a strong diagnostic response using its own organizational framework (three-layer split, lifecycle separation). Assertions test concept presence, not ICM-specific terminology
With-skill pass rate is 100% across all evals (unchanged from iteration 1)
Token usage: with-skill runs now use MORE tokens on average (45.9K vs 36.0K) — this reverses the iteration 1 finding. Likely driven by richer skill content producing longer responses

The 6 fixes (one-fact-one-location, sessions-start-clean, smallest-version, content pipeline 3-stage, AI-not-work anti-pattern, context-files-living-docs) are reflected in with-skill outputs but their impact on the specific eval assertions is limited since evals test structural patterns, not these specific principles
Eval 2 needs assertions that test ICM-specific depth (factory/product terminology, Layer 3/4 model, token budgets) rather than concept presence to remain discriminating
